blocks.html:
Wellness Playbook

Section 1: Defining Health & Wellness

This section is standalone. It should teach you how the course defines health, how the five dimensions interact, and how determinants shape outcomes in real life.

Lesson 1

Lock in exact health and wellness language so your written responses are precise.

Lesson 2

Trace how one wellness dimension can raise or lower another over time.

Lesson 3

Explain community health issues with determinants, not just personal choice language.

Section Success Signal

By the end of this tab, you should be able to explain one health issue using both a wellness dimension and a determinant of health, with clear cause-and-effect wording.

Quality check: your explanation names the determinant, names the impacted dimension, and states what should change to improve outcomes.

blocks.html:
Teach The Core Idea

Health is the target. Wellness is the process.

Start with exact definitions, then move immediately into dimensional interaction. The goal is applied explanation, not memorized labels.

Definitions

Health (WHO Definition since 1948): "A state of complete physical, mental, and social well-being and not merely the absence of disease or infirmity."

Wellness: "An active process of becoming aware of and making choices toward a more successful existence."

The 5 Dimensions of Wellness

Wellness demonstrates the need for "balanced" or "well-rounded" lives. To achieve harmony, we must pay attention to all dimensions.

Physical

Ability to move efficiently; healthy growth/care of body. Includes sleep, nutrition, activity, avoiding risky behaviors, and regular doctor visits.

Intellectual

Critical/creative use of mind. Learning new things, curiosity, seeing other points of view, and enjoying stimulating mental activities.

Social

Relating positively to others. Involves communication, respect, maintaining healthy relationships, and how you interpret treatment by others.

Spiritual

Understanding values/beliefs. Sense of meaning/purpose, feeling "at peace", and having a direct relationship between values and actions.

Emotional

Acknowledging and expressing feelings constructively. Self-awareness, asking for support, and handling positive/negative emotions.

1

Practice: Wellness Balance

Use lesson language right away. Pick the dimension under pressure, then justify the choice.

Teacher checkpoint

Checkpoint: Explain the interaction

Strong responses name both dimensions and explain how one choice or condition changes the other.

blocks.html:
Teach The Bigger System

The determinants explain why health outcomes are not just personal choices.

Use determinants to explain what is shaping outcomes, then pair each determinant with the dimension that is under pressure.

The 12 Determinants of Health

Ranked from most to least influential by the Public Health Agency of Canada:

  1. Income and Social Status: Health improves at each step up the income ladder. High income determines living conditions like safe housing and ability to buy good food. This is the most influential determinant.
  2. Social Support Networks: Support from families, friends, and community acts as a buffer against health problems and helps solve problems/deal with adversity.
  3. Education and Literacy: Health improves with level of education. It equips people with skills for problem solving, job security, and understanding health information.
  4. Employment/Working Conditions: Unemployment, underemployment, and stressful/unsafe work are connected to poor health. Control over work circumstances improves health.
  5. Social Environments: Social stability, recognition of diversity, safety, good working relationships, and cohesive communities reduce risks.
  6. Physical Environments: Contaminants in air, water, food, and soil causes negative health effects (cancer, respiratory illness). Housing quality and community design are key factors.
  7. Personal Health Practices/Coping Skills: Actions to prevent disease and promote self-care (avoiding toxins, eating well, avoiding drinking and driving).
  8. Healthy Child Development: Early childhood experiences affect brain development, school readiness, and health. Affected by housing, family income, and nutrition.
  9. Biology and Genetic Endowment: Basic biology and organic make-up. Inherited predispositions to certain diseases.
  10. Health Services: Access to services that maintain and promote health, prevent disease, and restore health make a significant difference.
  11. Gender: Society-determined roles, personality traits, attitudes, relative power, and influence placed on the two sexes.
  12. Culture: Can lead to marginalization or stigmatization, but cultural values can also alleviate negative influences.

Apply The Determinants

Determinant Insight Studio

Use these determinant snapshots as your reasoning bank when you answer scenario questions and assignment prompts.

Determinant 9

Biology and Genetic Endowment

The basic biology and organic make-up of the human body are an important determinant of health. In some circumstances genetic endowment can predispose certain individuals to particular diseases or health problems.

Determinant 10

Health Services

Access to health services can significantly improve a person’s health. In particular, access to services that maintain and promote health, prevent disease, and restore health can make a significant difference.

Determinant 11

Gender

Many health issues are related to gender-based social status or roles. Gender refers to the array of society-determined roles, personality traits, attitudes, behaviours, values, relative power, and influence that soci...

Determinant 12

Culture

Some people or groups of people may face additional health risks due to cultural values that may contribute to conditions such as marginalization, stigmatization, loss of or devaluation of language and culture, and la...

Quick sort: choose the determinant that best fits each scenario.

Section 1 Outcomes

What you should be able to do

  • Evaluate determinants of good health in Canadian society.
  • Compare recognized international and national definitions of health and wellness.
  • Describe the twelve key determinants of health with concrete examples.
  • Explain how determinants inform evidence-based health policy decisions.

Guiding Questions

Use these to frame your responses

  • What does it mean to be well?
  • How does society define health?
  • What is your personal definition of health and wellness?

Determinants and Public Policy

Why this matters beyond individual choices

Canadian public health uses a population health approach because outcomes are strongly shaped by factors outside individual control. Policy decisions are used to reduce inequities created by determinants such as income, education, environment, and access to services.

Assignment Handoff

Complete Section 1 assignment questions 1-3

Use your determinant reasoning and policy bridge notes above when you complete the assignment responses.

2

Practice: Determinant Check

Match each situation to the determinant doing the heaviest causal work.

Teacher checkpoint

Checkpoint: Bridge to the assignment

A strong bridge names the issue, links it to a determinant, and explains the most affected dimension with a cause-and-effect sentence.

Anatomy Command Center

Section 2: Inside Out - How the Body Works

This section should let you explain body systems, movement mechanics, and anatomy language without relying on source-page callouts.

Concept

Lock in anatomy, physiology, pathology, and homeostasis terms.

Systems

Connect each major body system to its core function quickly and accurately.

Movement

Use agonist/antagonist, muscle type, and directional language in real examples.

Section 2 Outcomes

  • Describe major structures and functions in human body systems.
  • Use anatomy terminology accurately when describing position and movement.
  • Explain how body systems coordinate to maintain internal balance.

Anatomy Guiding Questions

  • How do structure and function connect at cell, tissue, organ, and system levels?
  • What feedback mechanisms keep the body stable under stress?
  • Which systems must coordinate for safe movement and performance?

blocks.html:
Systems First

Anatomy Systems Studio

Use these cards as your fast reference set for system-level explanations.

Fundamental Concepts

Key Definitions

  • Anatomy: Study of structure and relationships among structures.
  • Physiology: Study of the function of body parts.
  • Pathology: Scientific study of the nature of disease.
  • Pathological Anatomy: Study of structural changes related to disease.

Homeostasis

From Greek "homeo" (same) and "stasis" (standing). It means maintaining a constant internal environment (temp, blood sugar, pressure) despite outside changes.

Negative Feedback: Like a thermostat. When a level goes off-balance, the body signals to correct it (e.g., shivering to warm up).

Levels of Structural Organization

  1. Cells: Basic unit of the body (approx 70 trillion). Example: Red Blood Cell.
  2. Tissues: Groups of similar cells working together. Example: Muscle Tissue, Bone Tissue.
  3. Organs: Different tissues working together to do a job. Example: Heart (made of muscle tissue, nerve tissue, etc.).
  4. Systems: Groups of organs working together. Example: Cardiovascular System.

Integumentary

Skin, hair, nails, sweat glands. Regulates temp, protects body, eliminates waste, receives stimuli.

Skeletal

Bones, cartilages, joints. Supports/protects, leverage, blood cell production, mineral storage.

Muscular

Skeletal, visceral, cardiac muscle. Creates movement, posture, produces heat.

Nervous

Brain, spinal cord, nerves, sense organs. Regulates activities via nerve impulses.

Endocrine

Glands (hormones). Regulates mood, growth, metabolism, reproduction.

Cardiovascular

Heart, blood, vessels. Distributes O2/nutrients, removes CO2/waste, acid-base balance, temp regulation, clotting.

Lymphatic

Lymph nodes/vessels, spleen, thymus. Returns protein/plasma, filters blood, immunity, transports fats.

Respiratory

Lungs, passageways. Supplies O2, eliminates CO2, regulates acid-base balance.

Digestive

Stomach, intestines, liver, pancreas. Breaks down food, eliminates solid waste.

Urinary

Kidneys, bladder. Eliminates liquid waste, regulates blood composition/volume and fluid balance.

Reproductive

Testes, ovaries. Reproduces the organism.

1

Systems Match Check

Match each function scenario to the best-fit system.

Teacher checkpoint

Checkpoint: Systems to Scenario

Strong responses identify systems and explain what each system contributes.

blocks.html:
Movement Application

Movement Mechanics Check

Convert anatomy vocabulary into explanations you can use in assignment responses.

Deep Dive: The Muscular System

Classification

  • Skeletal (Voluntary/Striated): Moves the body. Attached to bones. 650+ muscles.
  • Smooth (Involuntary/Visceral): Spindle-shaped cells. Found in organs, vessels, skin. Controlled by autonomous nervous system.
  • Cardiac (Involuntary/Striated): Found only in the heart. Cells branch and wave together.

Mechanics

  • Micro-Anatomy: Muscle cells (fibers) contain Actin and Myosin protein filaments that slide past each other to contract.
  • Tendons: Connective tissue connecting muscle to bone. Works like a rubber band.
  • Pairs: Muscles work in pairs (Agonist/Antagonist). When one contracts (shortens), the other relaxes (lengthens). e.g., Biceps/Triceps.

Major Skeletal Muscles (Diagram Reference)

Based on the course diagrams, here are the key muscles to know:

Ventral (Front) View
  • Muscles of Facial Expression
  • Sternocleidomastoid (Neck)
  • Trapezius (Neck/Shoulder)
  • Deltoid (Shoulder Cap)
  • Pectoralis Major (Chest)
  • Biceps Brachii (Front Upper Arm)
  • Rectus Abdominis (Abs)
  • External Oblique (Side Waist)
  • Sartorius (Inner Thigh)
  • Quadriceps Femoris (Front Thigh)
  • Tibialis Anterior (Shin)
Dorsal (Back) View
  • Triceps Brachii (Back Upper Arm)
  • Latissimus Dorsi (Mid Back/V-Shape)
  • Gluteus Maximus (Buttocks)
  • Hamstrings (Back Thigh)
  • Gastrocnemius (Upper Calf)
  • Soleus (Lower Calf)
  • Achilles Tendon (Heel Cord)

Anatomical Language

Anatomical Position

To describe the location of structures so everyone understands, we use a standard reference point. Standing upright, facing forward, arms at sides, palms facing forward.

Body Cavities

Ventral Cavity: Divided by diaphragm into Thoracic and Abdominopelvic cavities.
Dorsal Cavity: Contains Cranial (brain) and Vertebral (spinal cord) cavities.

Directional Terms

  • Medial/Lateral: Toward/Away from midline.
  • Proximal/Distal: Toward/Away from limb attachment.
  • Anterior (Ventral): Front.
  • Posterior (Dorsal): Back.
  • Superior/Inferior: Above/Below.
  • Superficial/Deep: Surface/Internal.

Planes of Motion

  • Sagittal: Divides body into Right/Left.
  • Coronal (Frontal): Divides body into Front/Back.
  • Transverse: Divides body into Top/Bottom.

Movement Terms

TypeTermDescription
AngularFlexion / ExtensionNarrowing / Increasing joint angle (Sagittal)
CircularAbduction / AdductionMoving away / toward midline (Coronal)
CircularCircumductionDistal end moves in circle, proximal remains stable
CircularRotationMoving bone around its axis
ForearmSupination / PronationPalm turned forward / backward
AnklePlantarflexion / DorsiflexionToes pointed down / up
AnkleInversion / EversionFoot turned in / out
ShoulderElevation / DepressionLifting / Lowering (shrug)
ShoulderProtraction / RetractionShoulders forward / backward
2

Movement Mechanics Check

Use correct muscle and position language for each scenario.

Teacher checkpoint

Checkpoint: Movement Explanation

Strong answers identify both muscles and describe the motion precisely.

Anatomy Assignment Handoff

Prepare your Section 2 response draft

Use systems and movement language from this tab in complete cause-and-effect sentences.

This section should help you make practical food, movement, and daily-choice decisions that you can defend with evidence.

Fuel

Apply food-guide decisions to real student schedules and constraints.

Movement

Connect activity guidelines to manageable weekly routines.

Decision Quality

Filter supplement and product claims using credibility checks.

Section 3 Outcomes

  • Use nutrition and activity guidance to build realistic lifestyle choices.
  • Evaluate health-product claims for credibility and safety.
  • Explain why one daily routine change would improve wellness outcomes.

Lifestyle Guiding Questions

  • What choice gives the biggest health return in your current routine?
  • How do you check if a nutrition or supplement claim is trustworthy?
  • What short-term action can you sustain for one full week?

blocks.html:
Lifestyle Foundations

Fuel Decisions Lab

Turn nutrition guidance into real daily fuel decisions you can defend with evidence.

Nutritional Choices (Canada's Food Guide)

Eating well leads to better overall health, lower disease risk (obesity, type 2 diabetes, heart disease), and vitality.

Key Recommendations

  • Eat at least one dark green (broccoli, spinach) and one orange vegetable (carrots, sweet potato) daily.
  • Have vegetables/fruit more often than juice.
  • Make at least half of grain products whole grain.
  • Drink 2 cups (500mL) of skim, 1%, or 2% milk (or fortified soy) daily for Vitamin D.
  • Eat at least two servings of fish per week.
  • Select lean meat and alternatives prepared with little fat/salt.
  • Satisfy thirst with water.
  • Include a small amount (30-45mL) of unsaturated fat (oils) daily.

Servings Guide (Teens 14-18)

GroupFemalesMales
Vegetables & Fruit78
Grain Products67
Milk & Alt3-43-4
Meat & Alt23

What is One Serving?

  • Veg/Fruit: 125mL (1/2 cup) or 1 fruit.
  • Bread: 1 slice (35g) or half bagel (45g).
  • Pasta/Rice: 125mL (1/2 cup) cooked.
  • Milk: 250mL (1 cup).
  • Cheese: 50g (1.5 oz).
  • Meat/Fish: 75g (2.5 oz) or 125mL (1/2 cup).
  • Eggs: 2 eggs.

Nutrition Tools for Schools: What to Believe?

Ask yourself these 4 questions when seeing nutrition info:

  1. Is the source professional? Look for Health Canada, Dietitians of Canada, or "RD" (Registered Dietitian). Be wary of "nutritionists" or "specialists" without credentials.
  2. Is it scientific? Good advice is based on research, not personal stories or testimonials.
  3. Is it current? Check dates. Info older than 5 years may be outdated.
  4. Does it sound too good to be true? There are no magic pills. Be cautious if they are trying to sell you a product.

Extension Studio

Fuel extensions

Use these source-aligned cards to strengthen your nutrition decisions with concrete examples.

Applied lesson detail

Lifestyle source expansion

Expanded lesson notes

Nutritional Choices

People choose particular foods for many different reasons:

  • convenience
  • cultural customs or preferences
  • lack of information regarding food choices
  • based on routine or mood rather than hunger
  • one person in the household does most of the meal planning,

shopping, and preparation

“Eating Well with Canada’s Food Guide” is a great place for accurate

information on healthy eating. Read through it now to discover what foods

are best for you, to learn how much food you need every day, and to find

out the benefits of eating well and being active every day.

Applied lesson detail

Lifestyle source expansion

Expanded lesson notes

Dannon Activia Yogurt

Jamie Lee Curtis might be enthusiastic about the

health benefits of Dannon's Activia line of yogurts,

but the FTC isn't. In 2009, a federal judge found that

Dannon's claims that a daily serving of Activia would

relieve irregularity and help expedite the digestion

process were totally unsubstantiated. It turned out

the company had been charging a 30% premium on

the "probiotic" yogurts over other brands when in

reality the contents in the cups were all the same.

As a result, the company was forced to pay out $35

million to consumers in a 2009 class-action lawsuit

and another $21 million to the FTC a year later, as

reported by ABC News.

Looking at the amount of misleading advertisements that pervade modern

media, consumers should be wary any time they hear that a product is

"scientifically proven" to work. Unless the advertiser specifically states that

its claims have been validated by the FDA, there's no reason to trust any

purported medical benefits of any piece of merchandise, whether it's shoes

or cold medicine. Often, these health and fitness products are just modern

interpretations of the snake oil that bankrupted early settlers in the Wild

West. So the next time you're tempted to buy a product that looks too good

to be true, just save your money – because it probably is.

What about Diet Supplements?

Peer influence, media, and family all play a huge role in your choices as a

consumer, including your health and wellness choices. One area where

young people might be more influenced by peers or media is the choice to

take supplements to enhance athletic performance or improve body image.

Nutritional supplements can be a very controversial topic. How much do you

know about common supplements?

Are energy drinks safe?

Four types of adverse reactions have been reported to Health Canada.

Energy drinks are considered a natural health product, and should be

consumed only according to the instructions on the label.

Applied lesson detail

Lifestyle source expansion

Expanded lesson notes

What are some common performance enhancing or body enhancing

supplements?

Sports drinks, sports bars, energy gels, protein powder, amino acids,

creatine phosphate, steroids, ephedrine, and caffeine are just a few of the

common supplements.

Are all supplements regulated by Health Canada?

No. Most sports drinks, sports bars, and protein powders are regulated as

food, some are regulated as supplements, and some are not regulated at all.

How can I tell if a supplement is safe to use?

Health Canada has information on supplements, or you can ask your doctor,

a pharmacist, or a sports nutritionist for information. Be careful about

information you find on the Internet.

Is there an easy way to monitor the safety of a supplement?

Food products have a nutrition facts table on their label. For natural health

products, look for a natural product number (NPN) or drug identification

number (DIN).

Will protein supplements make me bigger and stronger?

Excessive protein intake will not make you bigger and stronger. Research

shows that protein in excess of 2 grams per kilogram of body weight per day

does not increase the rate of muscle growth.

I am worried about my weight. Are meal replacement products safe?

While meal replacements technically are not unsafe, you need to ask

yourself two questions:

1. Do I really need to be worried about my weight?

2. Can I change my weight by eating healthy foods and being more active?

What is a healthy body image?

Healthy bodies come in all shapes and sizes. A healthy body image is when

you accept and feel proud of your natural body shape.

Why do teens choose to use supplements?

Often teens are unaware that the products they are using are supplements.

There sometimes is tremendous pressure from the media, peers, and

coaches to look a certain way.

With so much misinformation out there, what should you believe about

nutrition information? Read the following document for help.

Movement Planning

Movement Under Real Constraints

Build movement habits that survive workload, transport, family, and energy constraints.

Physical Activity Guidelines (Youth 12-17)

Requirement: The text recommends accumulating at least 90 minutes of physical activity daily. However, the CSEP Guidelines specifically state 60 minutes of moderate-to-vigorous activity.

CSEP Guidelines (The Standard):
  • Daily: 60 minutes of Moderate to Vigorous activity.
  • Vigorous Intensity: At least 3 days/week (Running, skating).
  • Muscle/Bone Strengthening: At least 3 days/week.

Benefits: Improved health, better grades, fitness, stronger bones, happiness, healthy weight, self-confidence.

Extension Studio

Movement extensions

Use these cards to design activity routines that still work when schedules are tight.

Applied lesson detail

Lifestyle source expansion

Expanded lesson notes

Physical Activity

According to the Public Health Agency of Canada, more than half of

Canadian children and youth are not active enough for optimal growth and

development.

Canadian girls are less active than boys, with only 38 percent of girls and 48

percent of boys considered active enough.

How much physical activity is recommended for youth? Children and youth

should try to accumulate at least 90 minutes of physical activity per

day and decrease the amount of time they spend on sedentary activities

like watching videos, playing electronic games, and sitting at a computer.

Review Canadian Physical Activity Guidelines for Youth 12 – 17 Years, which

sets out general recommendations for daily activity. Use the

recommendations to help get more active.

Applied lesson detail

Lifestyle source expansion

Expanded lesson notes

Here are some things that may help you to sleep better:

  • Set a regular bedtime. Going to bed at the same time each night signals

to your body that it's time to sleep. Waking up at the same time every day

can also help establish sleep patterns. So try to stick as closely as you can to

your sleep schedule even on weekends. Don't go to sleep more than an hour

later or wake up more than 2 to 3 hours later than you do during the week.

  • Exercise regularly. Try not to exercise right before bed, though, as it can

rev you up and make it harder to fall asleep. Finish exercising at least three

hours before bedtime. Many sleep experts believe that exercising in late

afternoon may actually help a person sleep.

  • Avoid stimulants. Don't drink beverages with caffeine, such as soda and

coffee, after 4 PM. Nicotine is also a stimulant, so quitting smoking may help

you sleep better. And drinking alcohol in the evening can also cause a

person to be restless and wake up during the night.

  • Relax your mind. Avoid violent, scary, or action movies or television

shows right before bed — anything that might set your mind and heart

racing. Reading books with involved or active plots may also keep you from

falling or staying asleep.

  • Unwind by keeping the lights low. Light signals the brain that it's time

to wake up. Staying away from bright lights (including computer screens!),

as well as meditating or listening to soothing music, can help your body

relax. Try to avoid TV, computer and telephone at least one hour before you

go to bed.

  • Don't nap too much. Naps of more than 30 minutes during the day may

keep you from falling asleep later.

  • Avoid all-nighters. Don't wait until the night before a big test to study.

Cutting back on sleep the night before a test may mean you perform worse

than you would if you'd studied less but got more sleep.

  • Create the right sleeping environment. Studies show that people sleep

best in a dark room that is slightly on the cool side. Close your blinds or

curtains (and make sure they're heavy enough to block out light) and turn

down the thermostat in your room (pile on extra blankets or wear PJs if

you're cold). Lots of noise can be a sleep turnoff, too.

  • Wake up with bright light. Bright light in the morning signals to your

body that it's time to get going.

Applied lesson detail

Sleep: The Vital Recharge

Sleep: The Vital Recharge

Most teens need 8.5 to 9 hours of sleep. Circadian rhythms shift in teens (melatonin produced later), making them stay up later.

Consequences of Deficit

  • Falling asleep in class (25% of students).
  • Poorer grades and athletic performance.
  • Drowsy driving (100,000 crashes/year in US).
  • Emotional troubles (sadness, depression).

Tips for Better Sleep

  • Set a regular bedtime (even on weekends).
  • Finish exercise 3 hours before bed.
  • Avoid caffeine after 4 PM.
  • Avoid screens 1 hour before bed (blue light).
  • Keep room cool and dark.
  • Don't nap more than 30 mins.
2

Constraint planner challenge

Choose the strongest movement strategy when life conditions are not ideal.

Teacher checkpoint

Checkpoint: Movement schedule defense

Strong plans include realistic timing, contingency logic, and measurable targets.

blocks.html:
Decision Quality

Supplement & Claim Forensics

Use a clear screening process before adopting products, advice, or routines.

Q&A: Supplements & Safety

Are energy drinks safe?

Four types of adverse reactions have been reported to Health Canada. They should be consumed only according to label instructions.

Will protein supplements make me bigger?

No. Research shows that protein in excess of 2 grams per kilogram of body weight per day does NOT increase the rate of muscle growth.

Are meal replacements safe?

Technically yes, but ask yourself: Do I really need to worry about weight? Can I change my weight by eating healthy foods and being active instead?

How can I check safety?

For natural health products, look for a Natural Product Number (NPN) or Drug Identification Number (DIN) on the label.

Extension Studio

Claim and product extensions

Use these cards to sharpen your credibility and safety checks before accepting product claims.

Applied lesson detail

Lifestyle source expansion

Expanded lesson notes

Being an Informed Consumer

One method of taking responsibility for your health and wellness is to

actively seek knowledge about health, wellness, and your body. You hear all

kinds of promises in the media that certain products will make you healthier,

thinner, stronger, more popular, and the owner of a generally improved life.

Can you believe everything that is presented to you via television, radio,

billboards, magazines, the Internet, and so on?

There are many examples of false or misleading advertising. Here is just a

quick sample:

Reebok's EasyTone Shoes

In 2009 and 2010, Reebok ran a series

of ads for its EasyTone and RunTone

shoes featuring lithe and toned models

professing the benefits of the footwear's

special toning soles. The ads claimed

that laboratory tests had found that

Tone shoes were "proven to work your

hamstrings and calves up to 11%

harder and tone your butt up to 28%

more than regular sneakers ... just by

walking!" Apparently, the special

uneven sole of the shoe forced you to

use more muscles when you moved.

The LA Times reported that an FTC investigation found the only thing that EasyTone shoes

actually did was make it uncomfortable to walk. As a result, Reebok was forced to refund

more than $25 million in purchases.

Airborne Herbal Supplement

For a while, Airborne seemed like a classic American success

story. The result of a second grade teacher's research, the

herbal supplement became a national phenomenon after it

appeared to finally provide the cure/prevention for the

common cold that science had yet to figure out. For 10 years it

reigned as the leading cold prevention supplement on the

market - and then the FTC got interested. According to

npr.org, a federal investigation found that the anecdotal

benefits of Airborne were just another example of the placebo

effect. In reality, the tablets did absolutely nothing to boost the

immune system or prevent colds. This incited a class-action

lawsuit against the company that Airborne finally settled in

2008 for $23.3 million.

Applied lesson detail

Being an Informed Consumer

Being an Informed Consumer

Consumers must be wary of "scientific" claims. Look for professional sources (Health Canada, Dietitians of Canada) and check currency (last 5 years).

Reebok EasyTone

Claimed special soles toned muscles 28% more. FTC found claims unsubstantiated. Refunded $25 million.

Airborne

Claimed to cure colds. Investigation found it was a placebo effect. Settled lawsuit for $23.3 million.

Dannon Activia

Claimed to regulate digestion. Charged 30% premium. Claims unsubstantiated. Paid $35 million.

Credibility filter: use this sequence before saying yes.

  1. Identify who is making the claim and what they gain.
  2. Check for evidence quality (research, not just testimonials).
  3. Verify recency and whether guidance still matches current standards.
  4. Decide if the claim fits your context, goals, and safety profile.
2

Claim forensics challenge

Choose the strongest evidence-based response for each product or ad claim.

Teacher checkpoint

Checkpoint: Claim credibility defense

Strong responses cite credibility, evidence, and personal impact.

blocks.html:
Risk Logic

Lifestyle Risk Tradeoff Simulator

Compare immediate convenience against longer-term health impact and build protective switches.

Extension Studio

Risk pathway extensions

Use these cards to map risk patterns and identify the strongest protective switch.

Applied lesson detail

Lifestyle source expansion

Expanded lesson notes

Sleep

How Much Sleep Do I Need?

Most teens need about 8 1/2 to

9 hours of sleep each night. The

right amount of sleep is

essential for anyone who wants

to do well on a test or play

sports without tripping over

their feet. Unfortunately,

though, many teens don't get

enough sleep.

Why Aren't Teens Getting Enough Sleep?

Until recently, teens were often given a bad rap for staying up late,

oversleeping for school, and falling asleep in class. But recent studies show

that adolescent sleep patterns actually differ from those of adults or kids.

These studies show that during the teen years, the body's circadian rhythm

(sort of like an internal biological clock) is temporarily reset, telling a person

to fall asleep later and wake up later. This change in the circadian rhythm

seems to be due to the fact that the brain hormone melatonin is produced

later at night for teens than it is for kids and adults. This can make it harder

for teens to fall asleep early.

These changes in the body's circadian rhythm coincide with a time when

we're busier than ever. For most teens, the pressure to do well in school is

more intense than when they were kids, and it's harder to get by without

studying hard. And teens also have other time demands — everything from

sports and other extracurricular activities to fitting in a part-time job to save

money for college.

Early start times in some schools may also play a role in this sleep deficit.

Teens who fall asleep after midnight may still have to get up early for

school, meaning that they may only squeeze in 6 or 7 hours of sleep a night.

A couple hours of missed sleep a night may not seem like a big deal, but can

create a noticeable sleep deficit over time.

Applied lesson detail

Lifestyle source expansion

Expanded lesson notes

Why Is Sleep Important?

This sleep deficit impacts everything from a person's ability to pay attention

in class to his or her mood. According to the National Sleep Foundation’s

2006 Sleep in America poll, more than one quarter of high school students

fall asleep in class, and experts have been able to tie lost sleep to poorer

grades. Lack of sleep also damages teens' ability to do their best in athletics.

Slowed responses and concentration from lack of sleep don't just affect

school or sports performance, though. More than half of teens surveyed

reported that they have driven a car drowsy over the past year and 15% of

students in the 10th to 12th grades drive drowsy at least once a week. The

National Highway Safety Traffic Administration estimates that more than

100,000 accidents, 40,000 injuries, and 1,500 people are killed in the U.S.

every year in crashes caused by drivers who are simply tired. Young people

under the age of 25 are far more likely to be involved in drowsy driving

crashes.

Lack of sleep has also been linked to emotional troubles, such as feelings of

sadness and depression. Sleep helps keep us physically healthy, too, by

slowing our body's systems enough to re-energize us after everyday

activities.

How Do I Know if I'm Getting Enough?

Even if you think you're getting enough sleep, you may not be. Here are

some of the signs that you may need more sleep:

  • difficulty waking up in the morning
  • inability to concentrate
  • falling asleep during classes
  • feelings of moodiness and even depression

How Can I Get More Sleep?

Recently, some researchers, parents, and teachers have suggested that

middle- and high-school classes begin later in the morning to accommodate

teens' need for more sleep. Some schools have already implemented later

start times. You and your friends, parents, and teachers can lobby for later

start times at your school, but in the meantime you'll have to make your

own adjustments.

Lifestyle source expansion

Expanded lesson notes

Wash your hands after

  • handling garbage
  • coughing or sneezing
  • blowing your nose or wiping someone else’s nose
  • treating wounds or cuts
  • returning home from school, work, or shopping
  • going to the toilet, helping someone else use the toilet, or changing a

diaper

  • having any contact with a person who is sick or with the immediate

environment of a person who is sick

Also, children should wash their hands after playing with toys shared with

other children.

How should you wash your hands?

1. Use regular soap (liquid or bar soap). Antibacterial soap is not necessary

and can promote resistance to antibiotics.

2. Wet hands with warm, running water and lather well.

3. Rub the hands together for about the amount of time it would take to sing

the song, “Twinkle, Twinkle Little Star,” (about 15 to 20 seconds).

4. Scrub all over, including the backs of the hands, the wrists, between the

fingers and under the finger nails.

5. Rinse under running water (for about 10 seconds).

6. Dry with a clean or disposable towel. If using a public restroom, use a

disposable towel to turn off the faucet to avoid further contact with the

tap.

When and how should you use hand sanitizers?

When soap and water are not available, alcohol-based hand sanitizers are an

excellent choice to use. Sanitizers only work to kill bacteria and viruses,

unfortunately they don’t work well if your hands are dirty or soiled.

1. Put sanitizer on the palm of your hand and rub your hands together.

2. Cover all surfaces, including fingers and wrists, and rub until dry (about

15 to 25 seconds).

Applied lesson detail

Disease Prevention & Control

Disease Prevention & Control

80% of common infections are spread by hands. Hand washing + Immunization is the best prevention.

Diseases Prevented by Hand Washing

  • Fecal-Oral: Salmonella, Shigellosis, Hepatitis A, Giardia.
  • Respiratory (Indirect): Influenza, Streptococcus, Common Cold.
  • Body Fluid/Contact: Typhoid, Staph, Epstein-Barr virus.

When to Wash (Before)

  • Handling or eating food.
  • Feeding others.
  • Inserting/removing contact lenses.
  • Brushing/flossing teeth.
  • Treating wounds or cuts.

When to Wash (After)

  • Handling garbage.
  • Coughing, sneezing, or blowing nose.
  • Treating wounds.
  • Returning home (from school/shops).
  • Using the toilet or changing a diaper.
  • Contact with sick people.

Hand Washing Protocol

  1. Use regular soap (antibacterial not necessary).
  2. Wet hands with warm water.
  3. Rub for 15-20 seconds ("Twinkle Twinkle Little Star").
  4. Scrub backs, wrists, between fingers, under nails.
  5. Rinse 10 seconds. Dry with clean towel.

Hand Sanitizer

Use alcohol-based when soap unavailable. Rub until dry (15-25 secs). Does NOT work if hands are visibly soiled.

Bloodborne Pathogens

Micro-organisms in blood transmitted via contaminated fluids. Main types to know:

  • Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV)
  • Hepatitis A (HPA)
  • Hepatitis B (HBV)
  • Hepatitis C (HCV)

  • Hepatitis Fact Sheets (CDC)

Regular Dental and Medical Check-ups

Part of healthy living involves proper maintenance of your body. Just as

most people who drive a vehicle schedule regular maintenance appointments

to keep the vehicle operating reliably and efficiently, you too need to

perform regular checkups to keep your body working properly.

Consider the following questions:

  • How often do you visit the dentist? The doctor? Other medical

practitioners?

  • What is your family philosophy on medical and dental care?
  • What factors influence how often you access medical practitioners?

Two determinants of health have a direct impact on how often an individual

might access medical care:

  • personal health practices and coping skills
  • health services

How often should you visit the doctor?

Although there is not an official recommendation, many medical

organizations, such as the Canadian Medical Association, Public Health

Agency of Canada, and World Health Organization, all agree that medical

conditions are more likely to be prevented or caught early with access to

regular medical checkups.

Applied lesson detail

Lifestyle source expansion

Expanded lesson notes

How often should you visit the dentist?

According to the Canadian Dental Association, how often you go for a dental

exam depends on your personal oral health needs. The goal is to catch small

problems early. For many people, this means a checkup every six months.

Your dentist may suggest that you visit more or less often depending on

conditions such as how well you care for your teeth and gums, problems you

have that need to be checked or treated, and how fast tartar builds up on

your teeth.

Ask yourself the following questions:

  • Do I brush twice a day with a fluoride toothpaste and follow my

dentist’s instructions on how to brush properly?

  • Do I eat a well-balanced diet, including food from all four food groups?
  • Do I have a history of cavities or gum disease?
  • Do I limit sweets and sticky foods?
  • Is my overall health good?
  • Do I floss every day?
  • Do I smoke?

The answers are all factors

that affect your oral health.

They will help you and your

dentist decide how often you

need to visit for checkups.

You should not determine your

need for dental care on what

your dental plan covers.

Protecting Yourself from Disease

Hand Washing

How important is hand washing?

Eighty percent of common infections are spread by our hands. According to

Alberta Health and Wellness, hand washing, in combination with

immunization, is the best way to prevent disease.

What types of disease can good hand washing prevent?

1. Diseases spread through fecal-oral transmission can be reduced by good

hand washing. Infections that may be transmitted through this route

include salmonellosis, shigellosis, hepatitis A, giardiasis, and enterovirus.

Because these diseases are spread through the ingestion of even the

tiniest particles of fecal material, hand washing after using the toilet

cannot be over-emphasized.

2. Diseases spread through indirect contact with respiratory secretions can

be reduced by good hand washing. Microorganisms that may be

transmitted through this route include influenza, streptococcus, and the

common cold. Because these diseases may be spread indirectly by hands

contaminated by respiratory discharges of infected people, illness may be

avoided by washing hands after coughing or sneezing and after shaking

hands with someone who has been coughing and sneezing.

3. Diseases may also be spread when hands are contaminated with urine,

saliva or other moist body substances. Microorganisms transmitted by

one or more body substances include typhoid, staphylococcal organisms,

and Epstein-Barr virus. These organisms may be transmitted from person

to person or indirectly by contamination of food or inanimate objects such

as toys.

When should you wash your hands?

Wash your hands before

  • handling or eating food or feeding others
  • inserting or removing contact lenses
  • brushing or flossing teeth
  • treating wounds or cuts

Applied lesson detail

Lifestyle source expansion

Expanded lesson notes

Immunization

Immunization against disease is another controversial topic. View the

following videos to learn Health Canada’s current perspective on the

necessity and effectiveness of disease immunization.

Immunize Canada-

Immunisation Canada

https://goo.gl/96HW7r

Immunize Canada -

Influenza

https://goo.gl/CAYSTm

Bloodborne Pathogens

Bloodborne pathogens are micro-organisms that may be present in human

blood. These micro-organisms can be transmitted to people who are exposed

to contaminated blood or body fluids.

Although there are many bloodborne pathogens, we will focus on these four:

  • human immunodeficiency virus (HIV)
  • hepatitis A (HPA)
  • hepatitis B (HBV)
  • hepatitis C (HCV)

Applied lesson detail

Checkups

Checkups

  • Dental: Usually every 6 months to catch small problems early. Depends on oral health needs.
  • Medical: Regular checkups recommended by CMA/WHO to prevent conditions or catch them early.

Public Health Agency of Canada (PHAC)

The role of the PHAC is to:

  • Promote health.
  • Prevent and control chronic diseases and injuries.
  • Prevent and control infectious diseases.
  • Prepare for and respond to public health emergencies.
  • Apply international research to Canada's programs.
  • Strengthen intergovernmental collaboration.

Roles & Responsibilities

Health Canada (Federal)

  • Sets national principles (Canada Health Act).
  • Delivers services to First Nations/Inuit.
  • Promotes health, prevents chronic/infectious disease.
  • Responds to emergencies.

Alberta Health (Provincial)

  • Administers health care insurance plan (AHCIP).
  • Issues Health Care Cards.
  • Funds Alberta Health Services (AHS).
  • Plans/delivers support to 3.7 million Albertans.

Health Care System Flow

  • Primary Health Care: First point of contact (Doctor, Nurse). Publicly funded.
  • Hospitals/Long-Term Care: Services paid by province; room/board paid by individual.
  • Home Care: Needs assessed/coordinated for comprehensive care.
  • Additional Services: Prescription drugs, dental, vision, podiatry. Not generally covered publicly (Private Insurance).

Complementary & Integrative Health

Shift from just curing to preventing. Integrative Health uses public and complementary systems together.

  • Natural: Herbal remedies, homeopathy, naturopathy.
  • Manipulation: Chiropractic, acupuncture, massage, reflexology.
  • Mind Cure: Hypnotherapy, meditation.
  • Folk Medicine: Aboriginal healers.

Caregiver Role: Abuse Reporting & Protection

Protecting Yourself (Boundaries)

You need to take precautions to protect yourself from being accused of inappropriate behaviour:

  • Strictly follow all rules and procedures for conduct.
  • Always maintain a professional relationship with people in your care.
  • If at all possible, never be alone with someone in your care, especially if the person is of the opposite sex.

Under the Child, Youth and Family Enhancement Act, it is a legal duty to report any suspicion of abuse.

Types of Abuse

  • Physical: Unexplained bruises, welts, burns, fear of contact.
  • Neglect: Hunger, poor hygiene, unattended medical needs.
  • Emotional: Aggressiveness, extreme withdrawal, depression, lying.
  • Sexual: Precocious knowledge/behavior, unexplained money/gifts.

Procedures

  • Do NOT ask for details or investigate yourself.
  • Listen and record facts in child's own words.
  • Report to supervisor, Child & Family Services, or Police immediately.
  • Look after yourself (debrief with counselor).

Confidentiality (FOIP)

Access only necessary info. Keep it confidential. Disclose only with consent, legal requirement, or if failure to disclose causes harm.

blocks.html:
Expanded lesson notes

SECTION 4: PUBLIC HEALTH

What roles, rights, and responsibilities for wellness are shared between the

individual and society?

Public health addresses the health needs of communities and individuals.

Two levels of government, provincial and federal, work together to provide

public health in Canada. The primary focus is on prevention and population

health needs rather than individual health needs.

The role of the Public Health Agency of Canada is to

  • promote health
  • prevent and control chronic diseases and injuries
  • prevent and control infectious diseases
  • prepare for and respond to public health emergencies
  • serve as a central point for sharing Canada’s expertise with the rest of

the world

  • apply international research and development to Canada’s public

health programs

  • strengthen intergovernmental collaboration on public health and

facilitate national approaches to public health policy and planning

Section 4 addresses the following outcomes:

  • Summarize the shared roles and responsibilities of individuals,

community, and government involved with health care.

  • Define terms related to wellness.

blocks.html:
Expanded lesson notes

Who is Responsible for Your Health?

Canada’s health care system provides universal coverage for medically

necessary health care services through a partnership between the federal

government and each of the provinces and territories.

Health Canada’s responsibilities include

  • setting and administering national principles for the health care system

through the Canada Health Act. This Act sets out the primary objective

of Canadian health care policy, which is “to protect, promote and

restore the physical and mental well-being of residents of Canada and

to facilitate reasonable access to health services without financial or

other barriers.”

  • delivering health care services to specific groups—for example, First

Nations and Inuit people

  • partnering with each province and territory to plan and develop

initiatives around health care human resource planning, medical

technologies, and the delivery of primary health care

In Alberta the provincial health ministry is Alberta Health. The

responsibilities of Alberta Health include

  • administration and delivery of health-

care services using transfer funds

from Health Canada

  • issuing health care cards and

administering the Alberta Health Care

Insurance Plan

  • planning and delivering health support and services to more than 3.7

million adults and children living in Alberta through Alberta Health

Services

blocks.html:
Expanded lesson notes

Complemenatry Health Care System

In the past, the focus of Canada’s health care systesm was to cure patients

of disease. However, in recent years, the focus of Canda’s health care

system has widened to include disease prevention. This shift has given rise

to a complementary system of health care within Canada.

Canada’s system of complementary health care includes alternative forms of

health care such as

herbal remedies, homeopathy,

and naturopathy

manipulation (osteopathy,

chiropractic, acupuncture,

acupressure, massage

therapy, and reflexology)

devices (ozone generators,

negative ionizers, radionic

machines)

mind cure (hypnotherapy,

meditation, mental imaging or

visualization)

folk medicine (Aboriginal

healers, Ayurvedic medicine)

Increasingly, the public health and complementary systems of health care

are used together to care for or treat patients or clients. This is referred to

as integrative health.

The two scenarios below provide examples of integrative health:

A medical doctor offers acupunture for pain relief or suggests mental

imaging in addition to or instead of conventional therapy.

A patient receives a diagnosis from a medical doctor of a specific

disease. In addition to receiving the treatment prescribed by the

doctor, the patient seeks an alternative practitioner who provides

supplemental therapies.

Note to Student: If you use supplementary therapy, food supplements and vitamins, treatments and/or

procedures recommended by alternative practitioners, or medications prescribed by another doctor, always inform

your doctor when you visit her or him about any matter of your health.

blocks.html:
Expanded lesson notes

Your Role as a Caregiver

Many of you are taking

this course because you

are interested in pursuing

a career within the health

care industry.

What are your legal

obligations as a person

providing services in

health care, community

support, volunteer, or

recreational settings?

As a caregiver you have access to private information about the people you

work with. Alberta’s Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy

Act (FOIP) requires that you should have access to only that personal

information necessary to do your job. FOIP also requires that you keep this

information confidential at all times and only use it for the purpose for which

you were given access.

You should disclose confidential client information only as necessary, with

consent, as legally required or where the failure to disclose would cause

significant harm to the client or others. The one exception to this rule is if

you suspect that someone is being abused.

Unfortunately, you also need to take precautions to protect yourself from

being accused of inappropriate behaviour. You should, for example,

  • strictly follow all rules and procedures for conduct
  • always maintain a professional relationship with people in your care
  • if at all possible, never be alone with someone in your care, especially

if the person is of the opposite sex

blocks.html:
Expanded lesson notes

What if You Suspect Someone Is Being Abused?

Children depend on the adults in their lives to care for and protect them.

They have a right to be safe from harm and neglect. Most of the time, young

people who are being abused will demonstrate signs of the abuse, even if

they never tell anyone. Sometimes a young person may display signs that

seem to indicate abuse, but really the signs are pointing out another issue in

his or her life. As a person in a helping role, you may be in a position to

observe or suspect abuse. If you know the signs and know what to do when

you see the signs and suspect abuse, you can help. It is the combination,

frequency and duration of indicators that should alert you to a

problem.

Type of Abuse Signs, Symptoms, & Indicators

Physical Abuse unexplained bruises, welts, lacerations, burns, or

abrasions

unwillingness to tell you how an injury occurred

extreme behaviours (from extreme aggressiveness

to extreme withdrawal)

lack of trust and fear of physical contact with adults

wearing clothing that covers arms and legs and

resisting changing clothing for any reason

Neglect appears hungry, listless and tired, has poor personal

hygiene and frequently unattended physical or

medical needs (hair, teeth, eyes and ears)

gets inadequate supervision or childcare

arrangements

“fails to thrive” despite lack of disease or

abnormality

Emotional Abuse displays a range of behaviours from aggressiveness

and being out of control to being withdrawn and

depressed

seeks constant approval and has an unusual need to

please adults

has unusual fear of consequences of actions – often

leads to lying

Sexual Abuse has detailed knowledge of sexual behaviour

inappropriate for the child’s age

exhibits sexually precocious behaviour, or, creates

artwork involving sexually explicit body parts

receives unexplained gifts or money

is reluctant to go to a particular place or be with a

particular person

blocks.html:
Expanded lesson notes

Procedures for Reporting Child Abuse

If a child discloses abuse to you, do not ask for details. Listen to the

information provided and record it as soon as possible in the child’s own

words. Be supportive and let the child know it is right to tell someone.

Reporting suspected child abuse can feel very scary. However, as a

caregiver, the Child, Youth and Family Enhancement Act states that it is a

legal duty to report any suspicion of abuse as soon as it is identified to your

immediate supervisor or to Child and Family Services. Each disclosure is

dealt with on an individual basis. The responsibility for investigation and

follow-up lies with Child and Family Services and/or police. Cases will be

prioritized and responded to by Child and Family Services and/or police

depending on the urgent nature of the report.

After making a verbal report to the school authorities, you may want to jot

down some notes: the date and place of your conversation with the child is

important, and details on what the child has reported using the actual words

the child used.

You may never know what happens to the child after you report. This is one

of the hardest things to deal with when you report abuse. It is important to

look after yourself and talk to a trusted friend or counsellor about your own

feelings when you are involved in child abuse disclosures and reporting.
